topic: FFI - Opaque Types and Incomplete Types in FFI

turns.content: I'm trying to wrap a C struct that has no public API, but I've been using extern type Opaque on nightly. Is there a difference between using an opaque extern type versus just storing *mut c_void with PhantomData?

There is a significant difference in stability and safety guarantees. On nightly you can declare an opaque extern type which keeps the size hidden, but that feature is unstable and not part of the stable Rust standard yet. The idiomatic stable approach uses *mut c_void paired with PhantomData to create a DST-safe handle without requiring unstable features. Using *mut c_void ensures your opaque pointer cannot be coerced into any concrete type on the heap side unless you explicitly cast it, which prevents accidental construction of the actual C struct from Rust code.
So if I use *mut c_void, can someone still accidentally dereference it to get the raw memory layout? Or does PhantomData do enough?

PhantomData alone doesn't prevent raw pointer manipulation, but when combined with *mut c_void in a newtype wrapper like OpaqueHandle<T>, you enforce ownership rules strictly. Any attempt to deref or cast this opaque handle into a concrete struct type will fail at compile time because the trait bounds won't allow it unless you explicitly implement unsafe code to do so. This prevents accidental construction of the opaque C struct from Rust while still allowing safe memory access through raw pointers when absolutely needed.
That sounds safer, but what if I need to pass this handle into a function that expects an extern type? Can *mut c_void work there?

Yes, *mut c_void works perfectly as the stable alternative for passing opaque types across FFI boundaries. Functions expecting an extern type can be made to accept *mut c_void since they only need raw memory access, not the actual struct definition. You wrap your *mut c_void in a newtype pattern like OpaqueHandle<T> and implement necessary methods to convert it to *mut c_void when calling into C code. This maintains safety while providing the flexibility you need for interoperability.
I see, so I should avoid using extern type on stable? But what about ensuring !Sized for the opaque handle?

You absolutely should avoid unstable extern types in production unless you're explicitly targeting nightly builds. For the !Sized constraint, your OpaqueHandle<T> needs to be defined as an unsized type by default since it contains a raw pointer which is inherently unsized. When you use PhantomData with *mut c_void, the resulting type remains unsized because the raw pointer itself isn't sized. This ensures that any attempt to treat your opaque handle as a regular sized value will fail at compile time.
Okay so my OpaqueHandle<T> should be unsized by default? And I just need to remember not to implement Into trait for it?

Exactly. Your OpaqueHandle<T> must remain unsized unless you explicitly add a Sized marker, which would defeat the purpose of hiding the opaque struct. You never want to implement Into or From traits that could convert this handle into concrete types because that would leak information about what C struct it represents. The goal is to make construction of the actual opaque type impossible from Rust code while still allowing safe memory access through raw pointers when absolutely necessary.
Got it, so I'll stick with *mut c_void and PhantomData, but how do I prevent users from accidentally creating instances that expose the struct?

You prevent accidental exposure by designing your API to only provide methods that work on raw pointers or external data. Users can still create OpaqueHandle<T> instances safely because they don't have access to the C struct definition in Rust anyway. The key is ensuring no public method allows conversion back to a concrete type, and you document clearly that this handle represents an opaque boundary. This makes it impossible for users to accidentally construct or expose the actual C struct while maintaining full control over what data gets passed across the FFI.

topic: FFI - Opaque Types and Incomplete Types in FFI

turns.content: What's the actual difference between using an opaque extern type versus a raw pointer like *mut c_void when wrapping C structs in my Rust tool? I'm trying to automate some legacy API calls and I keep getting confused about why my compiler is complaining that my wrapper isn't sized.

The core difference is that an opaque extern type allows the C struct's definition to remain hidden from Rust while still being usable, whereas *mut c_void just gives you a pointer without any guarantee of safety or size. When you try to use the opaque type directly in your tool, it fails because Rust requires all types to be sized by default unless explicitly marked !Sized. That's why your wrapper isn't compiling.

Wait, so I can't just declare a newtype and wrap the opaque type? If I do that, won't the compiler still see through it to the underlying struct and try to use its fields? Isn't that exactly what I want to avoid?

You're right to push back, but you need to ensure your wrapper type is also marked !Sized so Rust doesn't attempt to peek inside the opaque struct. The pattern is to define a newtype like OpaqueHandle<T> that holds either *mut c_void or an extern opaque type, and then use PhantomData to make sure it never implements any trait that requires knowing the inner layout. This prevents construction of opaque types while letting you safely pass them between FFI boundaries.
